text: The surgeon begins the procedure by opening the gastrocolic omentum, following port placement opposite to a right colectomy.
The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum, revealing the tattoo marking the location of the tumor, and continues the dissection towards the tumor site.
The surgeon observes significant edema around the tumor, making separation difficult, and proceeds with an en bloc resection of the stomach.
The surgeon observes the tumor adhered to the stomach at the greater curvature and proceeds with an en bloc resection.
The surgeon proceeds beneath the stomach, creating a window in the tissue to facilitate a wedge gastrectomy.
The surgeon opens the mesentery along the greater curvature of the stomach, near the fundus, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a reinforced green load stapler to transect tissue, continuing the resection process along the gastrocolic omentum towards the tattoo-marked tumor site.
The surgeon applies the final stapler to complete the gastrectomy, ensuring good perfusion to the remaining stomach tissue.
At the end of the video, there is visible bleeding from the staple line after the stomach has been resected.
The surgeon uses the final load of the stapler to achieve retraction, preparing to proceed with the next step of the surgery.
The surgeon mobilizes the descending colon using a lateral to medial approach, then retracts the greater omentum upwards.
The surgeon transects the left branch of the middle colic artery after mobilizing the colon, with visible separation of the vessel from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon observes a dilated section of the colon proximal to the tumor, indicating the presence of obstruction or pressure buildup.
The surgeon takes down the mesentery, creating additional space in the surgical field.
The surgeon aligns the bowel, preparing it for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon places sutures in an isoperistaltic fashion for retraction purposes, without tying them, and prepares to remove them shortly.
The surgeon places the stapler along the suprapubic area to perform an isoperistaltic side-to-side anastomosis, aligning the bowel segments for connection.
The surgeon removes the distal suture and repositions it more proximally to hold the common enterotomy, using the suture initially placed for retraction.
The surgeon places a suture on the proximal part of the common colotomy, tying it to use as retraction during the procedure.

The surgeon ties a suture at the bottom of the proximal colon opening, adjusting its position due to the larger size of the opening, and secures it at the crotch of the colon near the common collotomy.
The surgeon uses both proximal and distal silk sutures to gain momentum and cover the area, proceeding to close the common collotomy in a standard manner.
The surgeon begins with regular suture bites on the bowel and then switches to canal stitches, which effectively invert the bowel edges during closure.
The video shows the stomach and the pancreas, with the stomach positioned above the pancreas.
